expanded_description: A clear introduction to retroviruses for secondary school biology. This covers what makes retroviruses unique—they carry their genetic information as RNA but make a DNA copy that gets inserted into the host's DNA—and explains how HIV works, how it is treated, and why it is so hard to cure.

core_concept.principle: Retroviruses are RNA viruses that do something unusual: they make a DNA copy of their RNA and insert it into the host cell's DNA. Once inserted, the viral DNA (called a provirus) stays there forever, making new viruses whenever the cell is active.
core_concept.explanation:
Most viruses have either DNA or RNA, but they follow the usual flow of genetic information: DNA makes RNA makes protein. Retroviruses turn this around. They have RNA, but they make a DNA copy of it. This is called reverse transcription—going backwards from RNA to DNA.

When a retrovirus infects a cell, it brings its own special enzyme called reverse transcriptase. This enzyme copies the viral RNA into DNA. Then another enzyme, integrase, inserts this viral DNA into the host cell's chromosomes. The inserted viral DNA is called a provirus. Once it is in, it stays there forever. Every time the cell divides, the provirus is copied along with the cell's own genes.

The most famous retrovirus is HIV (human immunodeficiency virus), which causes AIDS. HIV infects and destroys a type of white blood cell called CD4 cells. These cells are crucial for your immune system. When too many CD4 cells are destroyed, the immune system cannot fight off infections. This is AIDS.

Retroviruses are hard to cure because the provirus is hidden in your DNA. Even if you stop the virus from making new copies, the provirus is still there. It can sit quietly for years, then start making new viruses again. This is why people with HIV must take medication every day for life.

But we have powerful drugs that can control HIV. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) is a combination of drugs that block different steps in the virus's life cycle. Some drugs stop the virus from entering cells. Some stop reverse transcriptase from making DNA. Some stop integrase from inserting the DNA. Some stop the virus from maturing. When taken correctly, ART can reduce the amount of virus in the blood to undetectable levels. People with undetectable HIV cannot transmit the virus to others (U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable).

Retroviruses are not just pathogens. They have also shaped our evolution. About 8% of the human genome is made of ancient retroviruses that infected our ancestors millions of years ago. Some of these viral genes have been repurposed for useful functions. For example, a gene from an ancient retrovirus helps build the placenta in mammals.

Understanding retroviruses helps us understand HIV, develop treatments, and appreciate how viruses have influenced our own DNA.

image.alt: A diagram showing a retrovirus particle, its RNA genome, reverse transcriptase making DNA, integrase inserting DNA into host chromosome, and the provirus being transcribed back into RNA
image.caption: Retrovirus life cycle: RNA genome → reverse transcriptase makes DNA → integrase inserts DNA into host chromosome → provirus → host cell makes new RNA and proteins → new viruses assembled. The provirus stays forever.

sections.introduction:
In the 1970s, scientists made a discovery that changed biology. They found that some viruses could do something that seemed impossible: they could copy RNA into DNA. This went against the central dogma of molecular biology, which said that information flows from DNA to RNA to protein. These viruses were working backwards—they were retroviruses.

Retroviruses are a family of viruses that includes HIV, the virus that causes AIDS. They also include viruses that cause cancer in animals (like Rous sarcoma virus) and one that causes a rare form of leukemia in humans (HTLV).

What makes retroviruses special? They carry their genetic information as RNA, not DNA. But when they infect a cell, they make a DNA copy of their RNA. This DNA copy is then inserted into the host cell's DNA. This is called a provirus. The provirus becomes a permanent part of the cell's genome. Every time the cell divides, the provirus is copied along with the cell's own genes.

This is a clever strategy for a virus. By hiding in the host's DNA, the virus can stay in the body forever. It can sit quietly (latent) without making new viruses. It can reactivate later when conditions are right. This makes retroviruses very hard to eliminate.

HIV is the most famous retrovirus. It infects and destroys CD4 cells, which are key players in the immune system. Without treatment, HIV eventually destroys so many CD4 cells that the immune system can no longer fight off infections. This is AIDS.

But we have learned a lot about retroviruses since the 1970s. We have developed powerful drugs that can stop HIV from replicating. These drugs are called antiretroviral therapy (ART). They don't cure HIV—the provirus is still there—but they can keep the virus under control. People on effective ART live normal lifespans and cannot transmit the virus to others (U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable).

Retroviruses have also left their mark on our DNA. About 8% of the human genome comes from ancient retroviruses that infected our ancestors millions of years ago. Most of these are broken and inactive, but some have been repurposed for useful functions. One viral gene, called syncytin, is essential for building the placenta in mammals.

Understanding retroviruses helps us understand HIV, develop treatments, and appreciate the deep connections between viruses and our own evolution.

introduction_image.alt: A timeline showing key discoveries: Rous sarcoma virus (1911), reverse transcriptase discovery (1970), HIV identified (1983), first antiretrovirals (1987), combination therapy (1996), U=U (2016)
introduction_image.caption: Retrovirus discoveries: 1911 (cancer-causing virus in chickens), 1970 (reverse transcriptase found), 1983 (HIV identified), 1996 (combination therapy), 2016 (U=U proven). Each discovery changed how we think about viruses.

key_concept.definition: Retroviruses are RNA viruses that reverse-transcribe their RNA into DNA and insert that DNA into the host cell's genome. The inserted DNA (provirus) becomes a permanent part of the cell. HIV is the most well-known retrovirus.
key_concept.context: Retroviruses carry two copies of their RNA genome. They also carry special enzymes: reverse transcriptase (makes DNA from RNA), integrase (inserts DNA into host genome), and protease (cuts viral proteins into their final form). The provirus is transcribed by the host cell's machinery to make new viral RNA and proteins. The provirus can remain latent (silent) for years, making the infection impossible to cure with current drugs.

image.alt: A diagram of a retrovirus showing the envelope, capsid, two RNA strands, and enzymes (reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease)
image.caption: Retrovirus structure: Envelope (with spikes), capsid (protein shell), two RNA strands, reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease. The enzymes are carried inside the virus.

sections.key_concept: Retroviruses have a unique life cycle: they attach to cells, enter, reverse-transcribe their RNA into DNA, insert that DNA into the host genome, and then use the host's machinery to make new viruses. Key enzymes are reverse transcriptase, integrase, and protease.

classifications.type: Reverse Transcriptase: Making DNA from RNA
classifications.value: Understanding reverse transcriptase explains how retroviruses convert their RNA genome into DNA and why they mutate so quickly.
characteristics: Reverse transcriptase (RT) is an enzyme that makes DNA from an RNA template. It has three activities: RNA-dependent DNA polymerase (makes DNA from RNA), RNase H (destroys the RNA template after it is copied), and DNA-dependent DNA polymerase (makes the second DNA strand).
RT has no proofreading ability. It makes mistakes frequently—about one mistake for every 10,000 bases copied. This is 1,000 times more errors than human DNA polymerases.
RT uses a special primer to start making DNA. For HIV, this primer is a cellular tRNA molecule that the virus steals from the host cell.
The DNA product is called a provirus. It has special sequences called long terminal repeats (LTRs) at both ends. These LTRs contain signals for turning the provirus on and off.

RT is a target for many HIV drugs. Nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) look like normal DNA building blocks but cause the DNA chain to stop. Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) bind to a different part of the enzyme and block its movement.
classifications.behavior:
Reverse transcription is a complex process. It starts when the virus is inside the cell. RT grabs a tRNA molecule from the host and uses it as a primer to start making a DNA copy of the RNA. It copies until it reaches the end of the RNA, then jumps to the other end and continues copying. This jumping happens twice. The result is a double-stranded DNA molecule with LTRs at both ends.

Because RT makes so many mistakes, retroviruses mutate very quickly. HIV mutates so fast that within a single infected person, the virus is constantly changing. This is why HIV can become resistant to drugs. If a person takes only one drug, the virus will quickly evolve a mutation that makes the drug ineffective. This is why HIV treatment uses combinations of drugs—the virus would need to mutate in multiple ways at once to escape all of them, which is much less likely.

The high mutation rate also helps HIV evade the immune system. The virus's outer coat (the envelope) changes constantly. By the time your immune system makes antibodies against one version, new versions have already appeared.

examples.explanation:
Imagine HIV inside a human cell. Its RNA genome is about 10,000 bases long. It needs to make a DNA copy, but it has no primer. So it steals a tRNA from the cell. This tRNA binds to a specific spot on the viral RNA called the primer binding site.

Step 1: RT starts copying from the primer, making a short piece of DNA. It copies to the end of the RNA.

Step 2: RT jumps to the other end of the RNA. This is possible because the RNA has repeated sequences at both ends (R sequences). The DNA has a complementary sequence, so it can bind to the other end.

Step 3: RT continues copying, making a long DNA strand. Meanwhile, its RNase H activity destroys the RNA template.

Step 4: RT uses the new DNA as a template to make the second strand. It starts from a special spot called the polypurine tract (PPT).

Step 5: RT jumps again to finish the second strand. The result is double-stranded DNA with LTRs at both ends.

This process takes about 2-6 hours. During this time, RT makes about one mistake per genome. This is why HIV mutates so fast.

image.alt: Step-by-step diagram of reverse transcription: tRNA primer, first strand synthesis, jumps, second strand synthesis, final dsDNA with LTRs
image.caption: Reverse transcription: tRNA primer → first DNA strand → first jump → second strand from PPT → second jump → dsDNA with LTRs. Errors happen often, leading to fast mutation.

examples.explanation:
HIV's high mutation rate is a problem for treatment. When a person takes a single drug, the virus can quickly evolve a mutation that makes the drug ineffective.

For example, the drug lamivudine (3TC) is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI). It looks like a normal building block (cytidine), but it lacks a critical chemical group (3'-OH). When RT adds it to the growing DNA chain, the chain stops.

But if HIV mutates at a specific spot in the RT gene (M184V), the enzyme changes so that it can tell the difference between the real building block and the drug. The mutant RT still works, but it no longer adds lamivudine. This mutation appears in about 2-4 weeks if a person takes only lamivudine.

This is why HIV is treated with combinations of drugs. Modern treatment uses three drugs from different classes. The virus would need to mutate in three different ways at the same time to escape all of them. This is very unlikely. When people take their medication consistently, the virus stays suppressed for years.

image.alt: Diagram showing how a single mutation (M184V) makes HIV resistant to lamivudine, and how combination therapy prevents resistance
image.caption: Drug resistance: Single mutation (M184V) makes lamivudine ineffective. Combination therapy (three drugs) requires multiple mutations at once, which is very unlikely.

classifications.type: Integrase: Inserting Viral DNA into Host DNA
classifications.value: Understanding integrase explains how retroviruses become permanent parts of the host genome and why they are so hard to cure.
characteristics: Integrase is an enzyme that cuts the host cell's DNA and inserts the viral DNA. It has two jobs: 3'-processing (removing two bases from the ends of the viral DNA) and strand transfer (inserting the viral DNA into the host DNA).
Integration is permanent. Once the viral DNA is inserted, it stays there for the life of the cell. When the cell divides, the viral DNA is copied along with the cell's own genes.

Integrase does not cut randomly. It tends to insert viral DNA into active genes (genes that are being used by the cell). This is good for the virus—it ensures that the viral DNA will be turned on. But it can also be dangerous—if the virus inserts into a cancer-related gene, it can cause cancer.
Integrase inhibitors are a class of HIV drugs. They work by blocking the strand transfer step. They bind to the active site of integrase and prevent it from inserting the viral DNA.

Modern integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, bictegravir) are very effective. They have a high barrier to resistance—the virus needs multiple mutations to escape them. They are now the first-line treatment for HIV.
classifications.behavior:
After reverse transcription, the viral DNA is still in the cytoplasm. It is part of a complex called the pre-integration complex (PIC). The PIC also contains integrase and other viral proteins. It must get into the nucleus to integrate.

For HIV, the PIC can enter the nucleus even when the cell is not dividing. This is important because HIV infects immune cells that are often resting. Other retroviruses cannot enter the nucleus of non-dividing cells; they need the cell to divide so the nuclear envelope breaks down.

Once in the nucleus, integrase finds a spot in the host DNA. It prefers active genes—genes that are turned on. It cuts the host DNA and inserts the viral DNA. The cell's repair machinery fills in the gaps, creating a permanent provirus.

Because the provirus is in the host DNA, it is very hard to remove. The immune system cannot see it. Antiviral drugs cannot reach it. It can stay silent for years, then reactivate and start making new viruses. This is why HIV is not curable with current treatments. The virus hides in a small number of cells (the latent reservoir) and can re-emerge if treatment stops.

examples.explanation:
Integrase inhibitors are a class of HIV drugs that block the insertion of viral DNA into the host genome. They work by binding to the active site of integrase and preventing the strand transfer step.

Raltegravir was the first integrase inhibitor, approved in 2007. It was a breakthrough because it worked against HIV that was resistant to other drugs. But raltegravir had a low barrier to resistance—the virus could develop resistance with a single mutation.

Newer integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, bictegravir) are much better. They have a high barrier to resistance. The virus would need to develop multiple mutations to escape them. They also have fewer side effects and can be taken once a day.

Today, integrase inhibitors are the backbone of most HIV treatment regimens. A typical first-line regimen is dolutegravir (integrase inhibitor) plus two nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (tenofovir and emtricitabine). This combination is very effective, well-tolerated, and has a high barrier to resistance.

image.alt: Integrase inhibitors binding to integrase active site, blocking strand transfer
image.caption: Integrase inhibitors: Bind to integrase active site → block strand transfer → viral DNA cannot be inserted into host genome → virus cannot replicate.

classifications.type: The Provirus and Latency: Why HIV Is Hard to Cure
classifications.value: Understanding the provirus explains why HIV infection is lifelong and why people must take medication every day.
characteristics: Provirus: The viral DNA that has been inserted into the host cell's genome. It is permanent. It stays with the cell for its entire life.
Latency: Some infected cells become dormant. The provirus is still there, but it is not making new viruses. These cells are called the latent reservoir.
The latent reservoir is the main barrier to curing HIV. The virus can hide in these cells for years, invisible to the immune system and unaffected by drugs.
Latent cells are mostly long-lived memory T cells. They can survive for decades. Even if all active virus is suppressed, these latent cells can reactivate later and restart the infection.
People with HIV must take antiretroviral therapy (ART) every day to keep the virus suppressed. If they stop, the latent reservoir will reactivate and the virus will return.
classifications.behavior:
When HIV infects a cell, it can follow one of two paths. In most cells, it starts making new viruses immediately. The cell is killed or destroyed by the immune system. But in a small number of cells, the virus goes into latency.

Latent cells are usually resting memory T cells. These cells are not actively dividing. The provirus is present, but it is turned off. The cell does not make viral RNA or proteins. It looks just like an uninfected cell. The immune system cannot see it. Antiviral drugs cannot reach it because there is no active virus.

These latent cells can live for decades. They are part of the body's immune memory. If the person ever stops taking ART, some of these cells will reactivate. The provirus will start making new viruses, and the infection will return. This is why HIV is not curable with current drugs.

Scientists are trying to find ways to eliminate the latent reservoir. One approach is called "shock and kill." Use drugs to wake up the latent cells (shock), then let the immune system kill them or use drugs to kill them (kill). Another approach is gene editing—use CRISPR to cut the provirus out of the DNA. So far, these approaches have not worked well enough to cure people, but research continues.

examples.process: The Berlin Patient: The First HIV Cure
examples.explanation:
In 2007, a man known as the Berlin Patient was cured of HIV. He had leukemia and needed a bone marrow transplant. His doctors found a donor who had a rare genetic mutation: the donor lacked the CCR5 receptor on their immune cells.

CCR5 is a co-receptor that HIV uses to enter cells. People with two copies of the CCR5-Δ32 mutation are resistant to HIV. When the Berlin Patient received the transplant, his immune cells were replaced with cells that could not be infected. After the transplant, he stopped taking HIV medication. The virus did not return.

This was proof that HIV could be cured. But it was a dangerous procedure—bone marrow transplants have a high risk of death. It cannot be used for most people with HIV.

In 2019, a second person (the London Patient) was cured using a similar approach. More recently, a few others have been cured. These cases show that curing HIV is possible, but the current method is too risky for widespread use. Researchers are trying to develop safer ways to achieve the same result.

image.alt: The Berlin Patient: bone marrow transplant from CCR5-Δ32 donor leads to HIV cure
image.caption: HIV cure: The Berlin Patient received a bone marrow transplant from a donor lacking CCR5 (the co-receptor HIV uses to enter cells). After transplant, his immune cells were resistant to HIV. He stopped ART and the virus did not return.

principles.principle: Reverse Transcription Turns RNA into DNA
principles.statement: Retroviruses are the only viruses that copy RNA into DNA. This allows them to insert their genetic information into the host genome, becoming a permanent part of the cell.
principles.application:
Reverse transcription is the defining feature of retroviruses. The enzyme reverse transcriptase is unique to retroviruses (and some other elements in our DNA). It has no proofreading ability, so retroviruses mutate very quickly. This helps them evade the immune system and develop drug resistance.

Reverse transcriptase is a key target for HIV drugs. Nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) act as chain terminators. They look like normal DNA building blocks but lack the chemical group needed to add the next base. When the virus tries to copy its RNA, it adds the fake base and stops. Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) bind to a different part of the enzyme and block its movement.

The high mutation rate is a double-edged sword for the virus. It helps it adapt, but it also limits how complex it can be. If a virus mutates too much, it can accumulate harmful mutations and die out. This is called error catastrophe. Some researchers are trying to push HIV into error catastrophe by using drugs that increase the mutation rate further.
principles.example: AZT (zidovudine) was the first drug approved for HIV. It is a nucleoside analog that looks like thymidine. When reverse transcriptase tries to add it to the growing DNA chain, it stops. AZT was a breakthrough—it prolonged the lives of people with AIDS. But resistance emerged quickly. The virus evolved mutations that allowed it to remove the drug from the DNA chain. Today, AZT is rarely used alone; it is part of combination therapy.
principles.analogy: Reverse transcriptase is like a photocopier with no proofreader. It makes lots of copies quickly, but many copies have errors. Some errors are bad for the virus, but some help it escape drugs or the immune system. The virus trades accuracy for speed and adaptability.

image.alt: Diagram showing reverse transcriptase copying RNA to DNA with errors (mutations) occurring
image.caption: Reverse transcription: RNA → DNA. No proofreading → many errors (mutations). This helps the virus adapt but also makes it vulnerable to error catastrophe.

principles.statement: Retroviruses insert their DNA into the host genome. This provirus becomes a permanent part of the cell and is copied every time the cell divides.
principles.application:
Integration is what makes retroviral infections lifelong. Once the provirus is in the host DNA, it is very hard to remove. The immune system cannot see it. Antiviral drugs cannot reach it. It can sit silently for years, then reactivate.

This is why HIV is not curable with current drugs. Even when antiretroviral therapy (ART) suppresses the virus to undetectable levels, the provirus is still there in a small number of cells. These are called the latent reservoir. If a person stops ART, the latent reservoir reactivates and the virus returns.

Integration also has risks. If the provirus inserts into a gene that controls cell growth, it can cause cancer. This happened in some early gene therapy trials. Researchers used retroviruses to deliver therapeutic genes, but the viruses inserted into cancer-related genes and caused leukemia in some patients. This led to the development of safer viral vectors.

But integration can also be beneficial. Endogenous retroviruses are ancient retroviruses that inserted into our ancestors' DNA millions of years ago. They make up about 8% of the human genome. Most are broken and inactive, but some have been repurposed for useful functions. For example, a viral gene called syncytin is essential for building the placenta in mammals.
principles.example: Syncytin is a gene that came from an ancient retrovirus. It makes a protein that causes cells to fuse together. In the placenta, syncytin helps fuse cells to form the syncytiotrophoblast, a layer that allows nutrients to pass from mother to baby. Without syncytin, pregnancy would fail. This ancient viral gene is now essential for human life.
principles.analogy: Integration is like a permanent tattoo. You can't wash it off. It becomes part of you. If the tattoo is small and hidden, it may not cause problems. But if it is in the wrong place, it could cause trouble. Most of the time, it just stays there.

image.alt: Diagram showing viral DNA inserted into host chromosome, becoming permanent provirus
image.caption: Integration: Viral DNA inserted into host chromosome → provirus → permanent. Copied when cell divides. Can cause problems (cancer) or be repurposed (syncytin).

principles.statement: Because HIV mutates so quickly, treating it with a single drug leads to rapid resistance. Using combinations of drugs that target different steps makes resistance much less likely.
principles.application:
HIV produces about 10 billion new viruses every day in an untreated person. With each replication, there is about one mutation per genome. This means that every possible single mutation arises many times each day. If a person takes a single drug, there will already be viruses that are resistant to it. These resistant viruses will quickly take over.

Combination therapy solves this problem. By using three drugs from different classes, the virus would need to have three specific mutations at the same time to escape. The chance of this happening is extremely low. When people take their medication consistently, the virus stays suppressed for years.

Modern HIV treatment uses combinations of drugs from different classes. A typical first-line regimen includes an integrase inhibitor (dolutegravir) and two nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (tenofovir and emtricitabine). This combination is very effective and has a high barrier to resistance.

Combination therapy has transformed HIV from a death sentence to a manageable chronic condition. People with HIV on effective treatment live normal lifespans. And if they are undetectable, they cannot transmit the virus to others. This is called U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable.
principles.example: In 1996, combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) was introduced. Before that, HIV treatment was one or two drugs, and resistance emerged quickly. People with AIDS often died within a few years. With cART, viral load dropped to undetectable levels, CD4 cells recovered, and people started living much longer. The death rate from AIDS in the US dropped by 70% in just a few years. This was one of the greatest successes in modern medicine.
principles.analogy: Combination therapy is like using multiple locks on a door. If you have one lock, a thief might have a key. If you have three different locks, the thief would need three keys at once. That is much harder. HIV is the thief, and the drugs are the locks.

image.alt: Diagram showing single drug (resistance quickly), dual drug (resistance slower), triple drug (resistance very rare)
image.caption: Combination therapy: One drug → resistance in weeks. Two drugs → resistance in months. Three drugs → resistance rare with good adherence. Modern HIV treatment uses three drugs from different classes.

components.definition: Retroviruses have three key enzymes: reverse transcriptase (makes DNA from RNA), integrase (inserts DNA into host genome), and protease (cuts viral proteins into their final form). Each is a target for drugs.
components.interpretation: Each step in the retroviral life cycle is essential. Block any step, and the virus cannot make new infectious particles. This is why drugs that target these enzymes are effective. But the virus can mutate to escape each drug, so we use combinations.

image.alt: Diagram showing reverse transcriptase, integrase, and protease with their functions and drug targets
image.caption: Key enzymes: Reverse transcriptase (makes DNA, target of NRTIs/NNRTIs), Integrase (inserts DNA, target of INSTIs), Protease (cuts proteins, target of PIs). Each step is essential.

categories.description: Protease is an enzyme that cuts viral proteins into their final form. Without this cutting, new viruses are not infectious.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
HIV makes a long chain of proteins called Gag and Gag-Pol. These are like a long string of beads that need to be cut into individual pieces. The viral protease does the cutting. It cuts at specific spots, releasing the individual proteins: matrix (MA), capsid (CA), nucleocapsid (NC), and the enzymes (protease itself, reverse transcriptase, integrase).

Without protease cutting, the virus cannot mature. The new viruses that bud out are not infectious. They cannot enter new cells. This is why protease inhibitors are effective HIV drugs.

Protease inhibitors work by binding to the active site of the enzyme. They fit into the pocket where the viral proteins would normally be cut. They block the enzyme, so the long protein chains are never cut. The virus that is released is immature and cannot infect new cells.

Protease inhibitors were a major advance in HIV treatment. They were the first drugs that, when combined with others, could suppress the virus to undetectable levels. Modern protease inhibitors (darunavir) are very potent and have a high barrier to resistance.

examples.explanation:
Imagine a long chain of beads. Each bead is a protein. The chain needs to be cut into individual beads to work. The protease is the scissor. Protease inhibitors are like putting a block in the scissor so it cannot close. The chain stays long, and the beads never separate.

When HIV makes new viruses, they are released as immature particles. The proteins are still in a long chain. The protease cuts them, and the virus matures. The capsid proteins rearrange into a cone shape, and the enzymes become active. If the protease is blocked, the virus stays immature. It may still bud out, but it cannot infect new cells.

Protease inhibitors were a game-changer in HIV treatment. Before they were available, it was hard to keep the virus suppressed for long periods. With protease inhibitors, combination therapy could keep the virus undetectable for years. Today, protease inhibitors are still used, but they have been largely replaced by integrase inhibitors, which have fewer side effects and are easier to take.

image.alt: Protease cutting Gag-Pol polyprotein; protease inhibitor blocking the active site
image.caption: Protease inhibitors: Block the active site of protease → Gag-Pol polyprotein not cut → immature, non-infectious viruses. Essential for HIV treatment.

worked_examples.title: Choosing a Treatment Regimen for a Newly Diagnosed Patient
worked_examples.problem: A 35-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV. His viral load is 50,000 copies/mL, and his CD4 count is 450 cells/µL. He has no other health problems. He wants to know about treatment options. (1) What is the goal of treatment? (2) What class of drugs is recommended for first-line treatment? (3) Why is combination therapy used instead of a single drug? (4) What does U=U mean, and why is it important?

step_by_step.explanation: Explain the goal of HIV treatment.
step_by_step.calculation: The goal of HIV treatment is to suppress the virus to undetectable levels (<50 copies/mL). This allows the immune system to recover (CD4 count rises) and prevents progression to AIDS. It also prevents transmission to others (U=U). Treatment is recommended for everyone with HIV, regardless of CD4 count.

Current first-line regimens are based on integrase inhibitors (INSTIs). The most common is dolutegravir (DTG) combined with two nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs), usually tenofovir (TAF or TDF) and emtricitabine (FTC). This combination is taken as a single pill once a day. It is very effective, well-tolerated, and has a high barrier to resistance.

step_by_step.explanation: Explain why combination therapy is used.
step_by_step.calculation: HIV mutates very quickly. If a single drug is used, resistant viruses will emerge within weeks. Combination therapy uses three drugs from different classes. The virus would need to mutate in three different ways at the same time to escape all of them. This is very unlikely. When people take their medication consistently, the virus stays suppressed for years.

U=U stands for Undetectable = Untransmittable. People with HIV who are on effective treatment and have an undetectable viral load cannot transmit the virus to others through sex. This has been proven in large studies. U=U is important because it reduces stigma and gives people confidence that treatment works.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Goal: Suppress virus to undetectable levels, restore immune function, prevent progression to AIDS, prevent transmission. (2) First-line: Integrase inhibitor (dolutegravir) + two NRTIs (tenofovir, emtricitabine). (3) Combination therapy prevents resistance; HIV mutates too fast for single drugs. (4) U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable; people with undetectable HIV cannot transmit the virus. This shows how modern HIV treatment works.
worked_examples.concept_applied: HIV treatment aims to suppress the virus to undetectable levels. Combination therapy (integrase inhibitor + two NRTIs) is used to prevent resistance. U=U means people with undetectable HIV cannot transmit the virus.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: The HIV Life Cycle
sections.introduction: The HIV life cycle has several steps, each of which can be blocked by drugs. Understanding this flow reveals why combination therapy is needed.

process_flow.description: HIV binds to CD4 and a co-receptor (CCR5 or CXCR4) on the surface of immune cells, then fuses with the cell membrane.
process_flow.mechanism: The HIV envelope protein (gp120) binds to CD4. This causes a shape change, exposing a binding site for a co-receptor (CCR5 or CXCR4). The virus then fuses with the cell membrane, and the capsid enters the cell.
process_flow.result: Virus inside cell; capsid released into cytoplasm.
applications: Entry inhibitors (maraviroc) block co-receptor binding; fusion inhibitors (enfuvirtide) block fusion.

image.alt: HIV binding to CD4 and CCR5, then fusing with cell membrane
image.caption: Step 1: Attachment and entry. HIV binds CD4 and co-receptor (CCR5/CXCR4), then fuses with membrane. Entry inhibitors block this step.

process_flow.description: Reverse transcriptase copies the viral RNA into DNA.
process_flow.mechanism: Reverse transcriptase uses the viral RNA as a template to make DNA. It makes many mistakes, creating mutations. The result is double-stranded DNA with special ends called LTRs.
applications: NRTIs (tenofovir, emtricitabine) and NNRTIs (efavirenz) target reverse transcriptase.

image.alt: Reverse transcriptase copying HIV RNA into DNA
image.caption: Step 2: Reverse transcription. Reverse transcriptase makes DNA from RNA. NRTIs and NNRTIs block this step.

process_flow.description: Integrase inserts the viral DNA into the host cell's DNA.
process_flow.mechanism: The viral DNA enters the nucleus. Integrase cuts the host DNA and inserts the viral DNA. The viral DNA is now called a provirus. It is permanent.
process_flow.result: Provirus integrated into host genome; infection is permanent.

image.alt: Integrase inserting viral DNA into host chromosome
image.caption: Step 3: Integration. Integrase inserts viral DNA into host DNA → provirus. Integrase inhibitors block this step. Provirus is permanent.

process_flow.description: The host cell reads the provirus and makes viral RNA and proteins.
process_flow.mechanism: The host cell's machinery reads the provirus and makes viral RNA. Some RNA is used as genomes for new viruses. Some RNA is translated into viral proteins. Viral proteins include Gag (structural), Pol (enzymes), and Env (envelope).
applications: Drugs targeting Tat and Rev are in development but not yet approved.

image.alt: Host cell making viral RNA and proteins from provirus
image.caption: Step 4: Transcription and translation. Host cell reads provirus → viral RNA and proteins. New viruses are assembled.

process_flow.description: New viruses assemble at the cell membrane, bud out, and mature into infectious particles.
process_flow.mechanism: Viral proteins and RNA assemble at the cell membrane. The virus pushes through the membrane, taking a piece of it as an envelope (budding). The new virus is immature. The viral protease cuts the long protein chains into individual pieces. This makes the virus mature and infectious.
process_flow.result: New infectious viruses released; ready to infect more cells.
applications: Protease inhibitors (darunavir) block maturation, making viruses non-infectious.

image.alt: New viruses budding from cell membrane, then being cut by protease to mature
image.caption: Step 5: Assembly, budding, maturation. New viruses bud out → immature. Protease cuts proteins → mature, infectious. Protease inhibitors block maturation.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: HIV and the Immune System
sections.definition: HIV destroys the immune system by infecting and killing CD4 cells. The body fights back, but the virus mutates too fast and hides in a latent reservoir.

process_types.energy_profile: HIV infects CD4 cells, which are essential for coordinating immune responses. As CD4 cells are destroyed, the immune system weakens.
process_types.direction: HIV infection → CD4 cells infected → CD4 cells die → immune system weakens → opportunistic infections → AIDS
process_types.function: Understand how HIV damages the immune system and why treatment is essential.
goals: Explain how CD4 cells are lost
Describe the progression from HIV to AIDS
process_types.energy_source: HIV uses CD4 cells to replicate; the immune system tries to fight back but is overwhelmed.

primary_example.explanation:
When a person is first infected with HIV, they may have flu-like symptoms. The virus replicates rapidly, and the immune system responds. CD4 cells drop temporarily, then recover somewhat. This is the acute phase.

After the acute phase, the virus settles into a chronic phase. The immune system keeps the virus in check, but it does not eliminate it. The CD4 count slowly declines over years. During this time, the person may feel fine, but the virus is slowly destroying their immune system.

Eventually, the CD4 count drops below 200 cells/µL. At this point, the person is diagnosed with AIDS. Their immune system can no longer fight off infections that a healthy immune system would handle easily. These are called opportunistic infections. Common ones include pneumonia (PCP), a brain infection (toxoplasmosis), and a cancer called Kaposi's sarcoma.

Antiretroviral therapy (ART) stops this process. When taken consistently, ART suppresses the virus to undetectable levels. The CD4 count recovers. People with HIV on effective treatment do not progress to AIDS and live normal lifespans.

image.alt: Graph showing CD4 count declining over years without treatment, with ART shown preventing decline
image.caption: HIV to AIDS: Acute infection (CD4 drop) → chronic phase (slow CD4 decline) → AIDS (CD4 <200). ART stops decline, allows CD4 recovery, prevents AIDS.

image.alt: Common opportunistic infections in AIDS: PCP pneumonia, toxoplasmosis, Kaposi's sarcoma
image.caption: AIDS: CD4 count <200 → immune system weak → opportunistic infections (PCP, toxoplasmosis, Kaposi's sarcoma). ART prevents these.

process_types.type: Endogenous Retroviruses: Viruses in Our DNA
process_types.energy_profile: Ancient retroviruses infected our ancestors millions of years ago and inserted into the DNA of eggs or sperm. These viral genes were passed down to all descendants.
process_types.direction: Retrovirus infection of germ cells → provirus in eggs/sperm → passed to offspring → becomes part of genome
process_types.function: Understand how viruses have shaped our genome and why some viral genes are now essential.
goals: Describe how ERVs became part of our genome
Explain how ERVs can be repurposed for useful functions
Understand that most ERVs are broken and harmless
process_types.energy_source: ERVs are now part of our DNA; they are passed down through generations like any other gene.

primary_example.process: Syncytin: A Viral Gene That Built the Placenta
primary_example.explanation:
About 25 million years ago, an ancient retrovirus infected the ancestor of primates. It inserted its DNA into the genome of an egg or sperm cell. This provirus was passed down to all descendants.

Over millions of years, most of the viral genes broke down and became inactive. But one gene, called syncytin, was repurposed. It makes a protein that causes cells to fuse together.

In the placenta, syncytin helps fuse cells to form the syncytiotrophoblast. This is a layer of fused cells that allows nutrients to pass from the mother to the baby. It also acts as a barrier, preventing the mother's immune system from attacking the baby.

If the syncytin gene is knocked out in mice, the placenta does not form correctly, and the embryos die. This viral gene, which came from an ancient infection, is now essential for mammalian reproduction.

image.alt: Diagram showing syncytin protein causing cells to fuse, forming the syncytiotrophoblast layer in the placenta
image.caption: Syncytin: An ancient retroviral gene. Makes a protein that fuses cells together. Essential for forming the syncytiotrophoblast in the placenta. Without it, pregnancy fails.

image.alt: Diagram of human genome showing ERVs making up 8% of DNA, with some genes repurposed
image.caption: Endogenous retroviruses: About 8% of human DNA comes from ancient retroviruses. Most are broken, but some have been repurposed for useful functions (like syncytin).

sections.relationship: HIV and endogenous retroviruses show the two sides of retroviruses. HIV is a dangerous pathogen that has killed millions. Endogenous retroviruses are ancient viruses that have become part of our DNA and now help us survive. Both are retroviruses—the same family of viruses, but with very different outcomes.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding retroviruses. Knowing these terms will help you describe how retroviruses work and how HIV is treated.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in retrovirus biology. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss HIV, its treatment, and why it is hard to cure.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of the HIV life cycle with key terms: reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease, provirus, latent reservoir
image.caption: Key retrovirus terms: Knowing these helps you understand how HIV works and how it is treated.

key_properties.explanation: A virus that carries its genetic information as RNA but makes a DNA copy that inserts into the host's DNA. HIV is a retrovirus.
An enzyme that makes DNA from an RNA template. Found in retroviruses. Makes many mistakes, leading to rapid mutation. Target of NRTIs and NNRTIs.
An enzyme that cuts the host's DNA and inserts the viral DNA. Makes the infection permanent. Target of integrase inhibitors (INSTIs).
An enzyme that cuts viral proteins into their final form. Without protease, new viruses are not infectious. Target of protease inhibitors (PIs).
The viral DNA after it has been inserted into the host's genome. Permanent. Can be active (making viruses) or latent (silent).
A small number of cells where the provirus is silent. These cells can survive for decades. They are the main barrier to curing HIV.
A drug that looks like a normal DNA building block but stops the DNA chain. Examples: tenofovir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, AZT.
A drug that binds to reverse transcriptase and blocks its movement. Examples: efavirenz, rilpivirine, doravirine.
A drug that blocks integrase from inserting viral DNA into the host genome. Examples: dolutegravir, bictegravir, raltegravir.
A drug that blocks the viral protease, preventing maturation of new viruses. Examples: darunavir, atazanavir, lopinavir.
Combination of drugs used to treat HIV. Usually includes three drugs from two or more classes. Suppresses virus to undetectable levels.
People with HIV who are on effective treatment and have an undetectable viral load cannot transmit the virus to others through sex.
A type of white blood cell that coordinates immune responses. HIV infects and kills CD4 cells. AIDS is defined by CD4 count below 200.
The amount of HIV in the blood. Measured in copies/mL. Goal of treatment is to get viral load below 50 copies/mL (undetectable).
A co-receptor on immune cells that HIV uses to enter. People with a mutation in CCR5 (CCR5-Δ32) are resistant to HIV. The Berlin Patient was cured by receiving a transplant from a CCR5-Δ32 donor.
An ancient retrovirus that inserted into the DNA of our ancestors and was passed down through generations. About 8% of human DNA is from ERVs.
A gene from an ancient retrovirus that is now essential for building the placenta. Causes cells to fuse together to form the syncytiotrophoblast.
Mutations in the virus that make it less sensitive to a drug. HIV develops resistance quickly to single drugs, which is why combination therapy is used.
A state where the provirus is present but not making new viruses. Latent cells are invisible to the immune system and unaffected by drugs.

summary:
Retroviruses are RNA viruses that make a DNA copy of their genome and insert it into the host cell's DNA. This is called reverse transcription, and it is done by the enzyme reverse transcriptase. The inserted DNA is called a provirus. It becomes a permanent part of the cell and is copied every time the cell divides.

HIV is the most important retrovirus. It infects and kills CD4 cells, which are essential for the immune system. Without treatment, HIV destroys the immune system, leading to AIDS. People with AIDS die from opportunistic infections that a healthy immune system would fight off easily.

HIV is treated with antiretroviral therapy (ART). ART uses combinations of drugs that target different steps in the viral life cycle. Reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs and NNRTIs) block the copying of RNA into DNA. Integrase inhibitors (INSTIs) block the insertion of viral DNA into the host genome. Protease inhibitors (PIs) block the cutting of viral proteins, preventing maturation. Modern ART is very effective. It suppresses the virus to undetectable levels, allows the immune system to recover, and prevents transmission to others (U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable).

HIV is not curable because of the latent reservoir. Some infected cells become dormant. The provirus is present but not making new viruses. These cells can survive for decades and reactivate if treatment stops. Researchers are working on ways to eliminate the latent reservoir, but it is a major challenge.

Retroviruses have also shaped our evolution. About 8% of the human genome comes from ancient retroviruses that infected our ancestors millions of years ago. Most of these are broken, but some have been repurposed for useful functions. The syncytin gene, which came from an ancient retrovirus, is essential for building the placenta in mammals.

Understanding retroviruses helps us understand HIV, develop treatments, and appreciate the deep connections between viruses and our own DNA.
